sentences.text: Yes.
This priest made the only smart move he could make, and I'm very happy he thought of it.
He called a family friend, a Catholic family friend, who lived in another town who did have transportation.
He had a car and he had a motorcycle.
But the car happened to be broke down.
But he did have his motorcycle.
And he called him during the night.
He excused himself from the gang, and he made that call in the middle of the night to this guy and told him, could you please jump on your motorcycle and go into Miskolc and find me.
He just vaguely knew the address, but he knew something about my grand-uncle and -aunt.
And he was able to describe where I am and pick her out immediately before dawn, because they're supposed to come and get her.
So he agreed he will do that.
He got on his motorcycle.
And he arrived.
I was already asleep naturally.
It was around 2 o'clock in the morning.
I was woken up, and it didn't surprise me because those days, so often we had to get up in the middle of the night and go to the bombing shelter.
And I thought it is an air raid.
So automatically I got into my dress, and everybody's very quiet and subdued, but I thought because of the air raid.
And when we were going down, I was curving toward the shelter.
And they told me, no, you have to go outside.
Somebody's waiting for you.
But please be very quiet and don't ask nothing, no questions, just go.
I sensed the danger.
I had no more questions.
I just followed the instruction.
I recognized this guy.
He was familiar for me.
I didn't know him too well, but I knew my father knew him.
So I trusted him.
And I sat behind him on a motorcycle.
And he took me to his home.
Yes.
He was Gyula-- that's his first name-- and Toth-- T-O-T-H.
And this man put me to bed early morning in his home, in the meantime, contacted my mom, and told her I am in his house, and she better come as soon as she can.
And I think that was the only time my mom found out what was going on.
So she came, and I heard my mother and this Gyula explaining what was going on in the background, how he received his call from the priest, and explained the situation.
And there was no time to waste.
And she had to come immediately and now do something, because he really doesn't want to be responsible, because now they're going to look for me.

And it turned out 6 o'clock in the morning, the Miskolc Police did arrive to my uncle's and aunt's house, and they were looking for me, and they were quite angry with my uncle and aunts when they find out I am not there.
And after that, it was a big search going on to locate me.
I remember I was even in a newspaper.
There was an article in a newspaper.
And I always kind of felt famous.
And I took it quite lightly.
Especially, I liked the description of me.
And they said brown eyes, brown hair, and she looks older than her age.
And this really pleased me.
[LAUGHS] I was 12 year old.
But I was acting older, yes, as most of the 12-year-olds of that day, yeah.
So that was my first escape.
And my mother really didn't know, first, what to do.
The only thing she knew, the bigger the city, the safest to hide.
But to get to the big city, you would have had to go by train.
And the trains go only twice a day.
And it is pretty easy to supervise who is on it.
So she knew that would be not wise to take any train.
And there was-- only contact she could think of was a man in Miskolc who had a taxi company.
And maybe he would be willing to take us with one of his cars.
But then we took the danger of returning to the city they were looking for me.
But my mother was extremely wise.
And she figured that would be the last place they would look for me.
Because by now, they will looked for me at my home, at my grandmother's home, at any relatives they can think of, or friends.
But they wouldn't think she's dumb enough to go back to the city that I just escaped.
So we went back to the same city.
We looked up this guy who was very familiar with my family.
And my mother offered a tremendous amount of money to take us to Budapest.
And he did.
And I remember he was scared.
I remember saying it, on a way, I hope you know I have a daughter too.
And I am risking my family, and I'm risking my own life.
I hope you know that.
But my mother also knew he got so much money for his trip it should be worthwhile.

And he took us to Budapest into a family whose name was Kolonics-- Sandor and Teresa, also childless couple, who also were very sympathetic to the children who had to-- who was deported or who stayed in ghetto.
And they were willing to do any kind of help they could.
I don't know how my mother got that contact, because I never heard about that family before.
But they were willing to take me in.
And I stayed with them just for a short while.
The problem was Budapest started to be very dangerous as far as the bombing concern.
And this Kolonics family lived right next to the railroad station, the big international railroad station, which got bombed practically weekly.
And sooner or later the house could have been bombed too.
And I was really petrified during the bombing-- the experience to be down in a basement with all of those strange people screaming and crying and carrying on.
And I really had not-- have my family to comfort me.
And no matter how nice that family was toward me, it was still, I felt, very lonesome.
My mother didn't stay with me.
She practically dropped me off.
And from that point on, I realized my mother became like a courier, a courier between me, my father, and brother, between home, to make sure the house is safe and OK.
She was able to do all the traveling because she received an identification, which showed she's Catholic, both of her parents being Catholic.
Just because she had a Jewish husband, it did not make her a Jewish woman.
So she was free to go.
But her children were not.
So she used her freedom, and she was constantly on the road.

And I wouldn't be surprised if she did other illegal activity in her traveling and taking messages from different parts of the country from those people who were together my mother-- with my father, because she was constantly going.
And she came to see me maybe every second week or so for a day, and then off she goes.
In her next visiting day, Aunt Teresa told her how petrified I am from the bombing.
And she suggest, not because she was not willing to put up with me on a longer term, but maybe it would be better to find a more quiet, safer place.
So my mother started to look around.

And I am not sure, but I think Aunt Teresa recommended the next family, who  were called Hodi family, who had the habit of spending the summer months in a vacation, in a resort area, which was a very smart move.
Because during this restricted era, everybody had to report their address at the police station within 48 hours.
If you made any move in any place, the first step you must take to report to the police, even if you was not Jewish.
It was for everybody, for the whole population.
And the only exception for the rule were vacation places because people went to vacation not for 48 hours, but two weeks or possibly a months.
So it was one place I could be without had to register at least for a months.
So that was one of the major reasons my mother decided I will go with that family, who was willing to take me with them.
With a good lump sun money.
And they even supplied me with a false identification as one of their nieces.

And I was supposed to appear there as Edith Wizsoly, which luckily I didn't have to use on a daily basis because I just told everybody I have a nickname, and nobody called me Edith, because I was afraid I would slip.
So it was wise, and I used my little nickname, which was not Judith.
But within the family, everybody called me Botsy.
And that was my nickname.
And I told people in this new place my name is Botsy.
And just call me Botsy.
Yes.
Yes, it was the beginning of the summer season.
It had to be around May, the beginning of May.
And I stayed there.
I don't know how they arranged.
I guess they used my assumed name for renewing my stay for another month, because I did stay there for two or three months before I was picked up by the police, by the gendarme.
It was nice.
I was full of nice villas.
It had a section which was a rural country like.
But they had that vacation spot was a nice lake and very nice, elegant villas.
And they were nice people in the area.
And I had no trouble to make friends.
And I knew everybody in the street and befriended one girl, especially, who had a gang.
All of them were boys.
And they included me into the gang.
And we did all kind of unusual things together.
We decided we're not going to be afraid from bombing.
The town was never bombed.
But Budapest being so close, we had seen the bombing, and the bombers did circle around our town.
And when the air raid came, everybody went into the shelter.
And we had a meeting place under a big walnut tree.
And we all escaped from the families and met under the walnut tree and climbed up on the huge tree and watched the bombing of Budapest from there every time it was bombing.
And I would never tell the boys the reason I came here because I was afraid of bombing.
I decided I don't let them know.
And I was with them to the bitter end.
The town is Gyomro.
And that town is still there.
And just the first time, two years ago, I went there to see how it became.
And I find the villa.
And to my biggest surprise, I find that girl who was my friend.
Oh, yes.
Her nickname was Csopi.
And I didn't remember her name, but I did recognize the villa.
And I knew she was two houses down from that villa.
And then I went there and I saw the house.
I recognized the house.
But I said, if I ring the bell, what do I say?
Who am I looking for?
I didn't remember her.
I just blocked some of my memories out.
And it was just slowly coming back.
And I said, I have to see if she's still here.
But what do I say?
But I said, the heck with it.
I rang the bell.
Somebody's coming.
She opened the door, and I look at her-- hm, my age, hm.
She could be the girl.
And very improperly I say, who are you?
She was supposed to ask me who I am.
But I got so excited.
I said, who are you?
And then she introduced herself.
But it was her woman name, her married name.
It didn't mean nothing.
I said, what was your maiden name?
And she again say a name which didn't ring the bell.
And then she said, my nickname was Csopi.
Yes!
And then she looked at me.
She said and you are Botsy.
She knew right away.
And we kissed and cried.
And it was one of the most pleasant reunions I had.
She never knew.
But she did knew after the war before I came because I did wrote letters to her, and I did explain.
Besides, the whole town find out, I assume, when two gendarme came to pick me up.
That must have stirred quite a commotion.
And I wouldn't be surprised if everybody find out after that.
But even that wasn't very simple.
If you're interested, then I will tell you my story of how they picked me up.
My mother just happened to be visiting me.
And she was home, and I went to the beauty shop.
My beautiful long hair was cut down to short to try to change my image which was in the newspaper.
In a newspaper they described my hair going down to my waist and wearing braids.
So my mom the next day had it cut short.
And I had to go to the beauty shop often to have it fixed.
So I was there in my beauty shop.
And one gendarme came in and he said, are you so-and-so?
And I said, no, I am that other person who I was supposed to be.
And he looked at the hairdresser, and he said, when is she going to be done?
The hairdresser said, oh, at least a half an hour.
I was getting a permanent.
And the old-fashioned way had those iron clips over my head.
And she said, at least a half an hour or more.
OK, I'll be back.
I go next door to get cigarettes.
And he left.
Immediately, I pulled my money out-- more what I was supposed to pay for the services.
And I said to the lady, I want to leave right now.
She knew it must have been trouble, and she was sensitive enough to cooperate.
And she took the iron out of my hair, and she let me go.
So by the time the police came back, I was no longer in that beauty shop.
I was afraid to go out to the street.
I didn't which way he went.
I didn't know which way I can run away.
So I went through the backyard, and I ran through everybody's backyard to reach the villa I was staying and to go back to my mom.
And when I got close to the villa, I saw the other gendarme was guarding my mom.
And there I was sitting under the tree.
And it took me time to make up my mind what should I do.
Should I stay in hiding?
Or should I walk up?
I was afraid they would beat my mom, to try to beat out of her where is your daughter?
She must have told them I am in a beauty shop, and that's how they came.
Now the other one coming back saying I am no longer there.
They're just going to beat my mother till she tells where my friends are or who is my friends.
I didn't know what to do.
I don't remember how long I was sitting there.
But once I heard my mom was crying and there were some screaming, and it scared me.
And I decided whatever happened, we do it together.
And I walked up to my mother.
By that time, the other gendarme came back and was already reporting me missing.
And that was the screaming all about between the two police.
One accused the other and et cetera, et cetera.
But once I showed up, they calmed down.
And they took me to the local police station, where I happened to spend the night till the local gendarme came to pick us up with a warrant.
And that night-- you know, come to think of it, there was a lot of good people in my life.
Because even in that police station, where I had seen the prison and it was a dungeon, was wet and very unkempt, and they decided not to put us there.
They took us, my mom and I, up in an office.
And somebody brought in a blanket, and they put it on the desk for me to sleep on.
My mom decided she's going to sit.
But at least they had a little feeling of humanity.
And you know, they could have put me into-- down into that terrible place full of cobwebs and bugs and everything.
But they did not.
And I was able to look out the windows.
And before long, all my gang came to the police station, and they were looking for us.
And I waved to them through the window.
So they stayed under that window.
You know, they spent the night on the sidewalk under that window talking to me and singing songs and entertaining me for the whole night.
I think I was just one of the lucky one.
And next day, the local gendarmes came, and they took me under their wing.
And we took off for another location which we didn't know.
They didn't told us where we going.
Yes.
That time we were both of us together.
But sooner we left a place, one of those familiar railroad crossing, my mom already assumed we are heading to the place that my father's labor unit is.
And it turned out to be it was that place.
The town is Jolsva.
And I knew my mother mentioned that's where my father is.
And in a way I was looking forward to seeing my father, no matter under what condition.
But I was hoping maybe we will have a chance to see each other.
But I never had the honor to see him.
We had a lot of interrogation-- very tough interrogation going on in your Jolsva.
Separately, my mother was interrogated.
And then I was separately.
And they really tried to break my spirit.
What they wanted to know, who helped us to escape?
Who helped us to do the things we were able to do.
And even though I knew some of the answers, I was a tough little kid.
And some of those incidents were actually funny because that was a professional interrogator.
He wear a leather jacket and boots, and he was a tough man.
And much later I found out he beat my father unconscious, and he was a brutal man.
And that's why we didn't see my father because he was in a hospital recuperating from that beating.
And he first took me lightly. 12-year-old little girl-- she would say anything I want to say.
And then one time, we were just at a conference room, very primitive, very simple, just ordinary desk table and some ordinary chairs.
We were sitting across each other.
And he had a pile of papers on one side of the table.
And he said, OK, now tell me everything.
What happened to you from the point of Miskolc when you disappeared?
Where did you go?
And I wasn't answering.
And then he said, OK, come on.
Look at all this paper.
We have recorded every step you did.
We know everything.
Then I said, then why are you asking me?
If you know everything, you don't need my say so.
So then he realized he had a little bit harder time as he assumed he will have.
In the mean time, he was playing with a whip, not a leather whip.
It was like a bamboo.
And he was just playing with it.
I think he was just working on my mind if he can scare me.
And once he had to turn to one of his assistants, and he put the whip on the table off.
And he was paging through something.
I picked up the whip, and I broke it to half.
Didn't said one single word, just broke it and put it back.
He got the message.
And after that he really-- very-- he was very mean.
And one of the things he said was, OK, you are playing tough.
Your father played tough, too.
But now he's dead.
And that was enough to break my spirit.
And they me collapsing.
And there were two gendarmes who were behind this man.
And one of them looked at me, and he said, waved to his head, no.
